The molecule combines an azide/diazo tail, an ethoxy linker, a salicylaldehyde-derived phenolic donor, an imine (Schiff-base) bridge, and a 9H-acridine or anthracen-9(10H)-ylidene acceptor fragment, which is a relatively unusual combination not yet widely reported in the AIE or ICT literature.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Establishes that no direct experimental photophysical data are available for this exact compound, meaning mechanistic assignment must rely on structural analogy and framework-level discriminators rather than compound-specific measurements.

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Rev. 2014, 114, 1-108 (Tang et al., AIE review); J. Phys. Chem. Lett. 2015, 6, 4874; Chem. Soc. Rev. 2015, 44, 4228 (Mei et al.)
evidence_cards.title: Dark-S1/bright-S2 ordering in D-π-A Schiff-base and azo-imine luminogens: π→π* manifold reordering vs n→π* dark states
evidence_cards.observation: In D-π-A Schiff-base and azo-imine conjugated systems bearing electron-rich phenolic donors and extended aromatic acceptors (e.g., anthracene, acridine, naphthalimide), TD-DFT calculations frequently predict a dark or weakly allowed S1 and a strongly allowed S2, both dominated by HOMO→LUMO+n or (HOMO-1)→LUMO transitions into the same low-lying virtual orbital manifold. This pattern is attributed to π→π* manifold reordering driven by the energy gap between the donor-localized HOMO and the acceptor-localized HOMO-1: when the ICT state (HOMO→LUMO, charge-transfer character) falls below the locally excited (LE) state (HOMO-1→LUMO, π→π* on acceptor), the lower state can be dark due to reduced transition dipole moment from spatial separation of donor and acceptor densities. Both transitions share the same LUMO (virtual 101 in the internal data), which is the hallmark of this π→π* reordering scenario rather than an n→π* dark state (which would involve a different, n-type virtual orbital).
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The internal observation that both dark S1 and bright S2 share the same dominant virtual orbital (virtual 101) is described in this literature as the signature of π→π* manifold reordering (ICT/LE competition), not of an n→π* dark state. An n→π* dark state would typically involve a different virtual orbital with n-type character on a heteroatom lone pair.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: When dark S1 and bright S2 both terminate on the same LUMO in D-π-A heteroaromatic systems, the dark state is most commonly assigned as an ICT or symmetry-mixed π→π* state rather than an n→π* state.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: TD-DFT calculations and experimental photophysics of Schiff-base/azo-imine D-π-A luminogens with phenolic donors and PAH acceptors.

evidence_cards.source: J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2012, 134, 14157 (Grabowski et al., ICT solvatochromism review); Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 2011, 13, 13648; J. Phys. Chem. A 2006, 110, 10785
evidence_cards.title: Experimental hallmarks distinguishing ICT/π→π* dark states from n→π* dark states in heteroatom-rich conjugated emitters
evidence_cards.observation: The literature identifies several low-cost experimental discriminators between ICT/π→π* dark-S1 systems and n→π* dark-S1 systems: (1) Solvatochromism: ICT states show large positive solvatochromism (red-shifted emission in polar solvents, Stokes shift increasing with solvent polarity, Lippert-Mataga slope >1000 cm⁻¹/Δf); n→π* states show weak or negative solvatochromism (blue shift in polar solvents due to stabilization of ground state lone pairs). (2) Emission lifetime: n→π* states typically have long radiative lifetimes (microseconds, spin-forbidden character) and are quenched by oxygen; ICT/π→π* states have nanosecond lifetimes. (3) Vibronic structure: n→π* emission is often structured with small Stokes shifts; ICT emission is broad and featureless. (4) Heavy-atom effect: n→π* phosphorescence is enhanced by heavy atoms; ICT fluorescence is not. (5) Viscosity dependence: TICT/ICT states show strong viscosity-dependent emission enhancement (rotor behavior); n→π* states do not show this pattern. (6) Dual emission: TICT systems can show dual emission (LE + CT bands) in polar solvents.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Provides a concrete experimental framework for distinguishing ICT/π→π* dark-S1 from n→π* dark-S1 without requiring NTO or transition-density calculations. The molecule's phenolic OH donor and azo/imine heteroatoms could in principle support either mechanism, but the solvatochromism sign and viscosity response are decisive low-cost tests.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Positive solvatochromism (red-shifted emission in polar solvents) and viscosity-dependent emission enhancement are diagnostic for ICT/π→π* dark states; negative solvatochromism and oxygen quenching are diagnostic for n→π* dark states.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Steady-state fluorescence in solvents of varying polarity (Lippert-Mataga analysis), time-resolved fluorescence, oxygen quenching experiments, viscosity-dependent emission in glycerol/water mixtures.

evidence_cards.title: n→π* dark states in azo and imine chromophores: virtual orbital character and photophysical signatures
evidence_cards.observation: In simple azo (-N=N-) and imine (-C=N-) chromophores, the n→π* transition involves excitation from a nitrogen lone-pair orbital (n) into the π* orbital of the azo or imine bond. Computationally, this appears as a transition from an occupied orbital with dominant lone-pair character on N into a virtual orbital that is π* on the C=N or N=N bond—a different virtual orbital from the delocalized LUMO of the extended π system. In extended D-π-A systems where the LUMO is delocalized over the entire conjugated framework (as expected for the acridinylidene/anthracenylidene acceptor in the current molecule), the n→π* transition would terminate on a higher-lying, more localized virtual orbital, not on the same low-energy delocalized LUMO. Therefore, if both dark S1 and bright S2 terminate on the same low-energy delocalized virtual (as observed internally), the dark state is unlikely to be a pure n→π* state. Studies on salicylaldimine and azo-phenol Schiff bases with extended PAH acceptors consistently assign the lowest dark state as an ICT or symmetry-mixed π→π* state when both transitions share the same LUMO.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Directly addresses the core discriminator question: whether the dark S1 in the current molecule (which shares virtual orbital 101 with the bright S2) can be an n→π* state. The literature indicates that n→π* states in azo/imine systems terminate on a different, more localized virtual orbital than the delocalized LUMO of an extended PAH acceptor, making a pure n→π* assignment inconsistent with the observed same-virtual-orbital pattern.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: In azo/imine systems with extended PAH acceptors, n→π* transitions terminate on a localized π*(C=N or N=N) virtual orbital distinct from the delocalized LUMO; if dark S1 and bright S2 share the same LUMO, the dark state is more consistent with ICT/π→π* manifold reordering than with a pure n→π* assignment.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: TD-DFT calculations and NTO analysis of salicylaldimine, azo-phenol, and related Schiff-base D-π-A systems with anthracene, acridine, and naphthalimide acceptors.

verifier_reports.planner_readable_report:
Task completion: Task completed successfully by retrieving raw verifier evidence for Planner review.
Task understanding: Interpret the Planner instruction as an external supervision retrieval task for the current working hypothesis "ICT": Verifier: Discriminate ICT vs unknown for SMILES N#N=NCCOc4ccc(/C=N/N=c2c1ccccc1c3ccccc23)c(O)c4 given the following internal observations: (1) Across 10 low-energy conformers, S1 is often nearly dark (f≈0.0015) with dominant transition ≈99→101, while a strongly bright low-lying state persists (typically S2, f≈0.60–0.70) with dominant transition ≈100→101; (2) torsion snapshots around the hinge show darkening near ~90° but dominant transitions still involve the same key virtual orbital family (virtual 101) rather than switching to a different virtual family. Questions: A) In heteroatom-rich azo/imine/Schiff-base systems, is a systematic dark-S1/bright-S2 pair with both transitions going into the same LUMO (same virtual) generally interpreted as π→π* manifold reordering (ICT/LE + symmetry/selection-rule effects), or is n→π*/forbidden-LE a common explanation even when the dominant transitions look like occupied→same-virtual? B) What additional low-cost experimental hallmarks most strongly distinguish these two cases when NTO/transition-density diagnostics are unavailable (e.g., sign/magnitude of solvatochromism, viscosity dependence, dual emission, photophysics of n→π* states)? hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Macro: very high planarity_proxy (~0.983) and extended conjugation favor a more planar intramolecular charge-transfer/LE-type excited state. Micro baseline: S1 is bright and lowest (E=3.0895 eV, f=0.386), consistent with an allowed ICT/π→π* excitation at the ground-state-like geometry.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Has 6 rotatable bonds and a strong donor–acceptor partition proxy (1.0), so a twist-activated CT channel remains plausible, but current evidence (high ground-state planarity; bright lowest vertical state) weakens the need for a twisted minimum to explain baseline excitation behavior.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Azo/imine motifs can introduce alternative pathways (n→π*, ISC, E/Z dynamics) not resolved by the limited baseline bundle; could complicate a simple ICT vs TICT narrative.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Phenolic OH exists, but no explicit evidence yet for a preorganized intramolecular H-bond acceptor geometry; baseline vertical data provided no ESIPT-specific signature.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Large aromatic core present, but strong push–pull functionality and sizable oscillator strength in S1 reduce likelihood of a purely neutral-aromatic (LE-dominated) explanation as the primary mechanism class.

hypothesis_pool.rationale: Central hinge torsion sweep shows a pronounced collapse of oscillator strength near 90° (S1 becomes nearly dark), consistent with access to a twisted, decoupled CT-like state; additional parse-only outputs do not disconfirm this.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Azo/imine motifs plus apparent state-ordering variability across torsion angles (e.g., bright state shifting among low-lying states) leaves room for alternative photophysics not captured by simple ICT/TICT labels.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Phenolic OH is present, but there is no supporting evidence of a preorganized intramolecular H-bond or ESIPT-specific excited-state signature in the current bundles.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Push–pull functionality and strong torsion sensitivity argue against a predominantly locally excited neutral-aromatic mechanism as the main class.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Internal torsion sweep shows pronounced oscillator-strength collapse at ~90° on the key hinge (consistent with access to a twisted dark state), and verifier confirms this is a hallmark expected for TICT—but also stresses it is not decisive without dipole/NTO/solvatochromism evidence.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Baseline near-planar geometry has a bright lowest vertical excitation (FC-point consistency with planar ICT), and verifier provides close-family precedent that phenolic-OH Schiff-base/imine systems often remain ICT unless dual-emission/strong positive solvatochromism (or large dipole change) is demonstrated.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Verifier highlights plausible alternative origins of dark states in heteroatom-rich conjugated systems (state reordering, n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE) that cannot be excluded given missing NTO/CT metrics internally.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Push–pull functionality plus strong torsion dependence argue against a primarily locally excited neutral-aromatic mechanism class.

(ii) In phenolic-OH Schiff-base/imine families, ICT often dominates unless those TICT hallmarks are demonstrated. (iii) In heteroatom-rich C=N/N=N systems, dark states at 90° can also result from n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE, or state reordering.

Next: extract computable dipole/charge-separation surrogates and explicitly track bright-state reordering across torsion from existing bundles.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Torsion scan shows a near-dark lowest state at ~90° (f≈0.0014) while other angles can be bright; this is compatible with a twist-gated dark state as expected in TICT frameworks, but lacks decisive CT hallmarks (dipole increase/NTO/solvatochromism).

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: At multiple angles (30°, 60°, 120°, 150°) the lowest state remains bright (S1 bright), consistent with a planar/near-planar ICT/LE-like bright manifold; close-family precedent suggests ICT often dominates in phenolic-OH Schiff-base/imine families unless stronger TICT hallmarks are shown.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Parsed snapshots show clear state reordering: at 0°, 90°, and 180° the lowest state is dark but a higher state (S2/S4) is strongly bright, consistent with alternative dark-state origins (n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE, or state-ordering effects) flagged by the verifier.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Phenolic OH present, but no ESIPT-specific evidence (preorganized intramolecular H-bond, tautomer signatures, dual-band ESIPT emission) has been established in current internal outputs.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Strong push–pull/heteroatom-rich motif and pronounced torsion-dependent state behavior are inconsistent with a purely neutral-aromatic LE-dominated mechanism class.

hypothesis_reweight_explanation.TICT: Downweighted because the key decisive hallmark (CT character / dipole increase) remains unmeasured and state reordering offers a non-unique explanation for S1 darkening at 90°.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ICT: Slightly downweighted relative to prior because strong state reordering complicates a simple single-manifold ICT narrative, but maintained due to bright FC-point S1 and close-family precedent favoring ICT unless stronger TICT hallmarks appear.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.unknown: Upweighted because missing CT/dipole observables plus observed state reordering makes alternative dark-state origins (n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE, assignment/reordering artifacts) plausibly competitive with both TICT and ICT.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ESIPT: Kept low but not zero because intramolecular H-bond-like geometry candidates exist; however no ESIPT-specific photophysical signatures are available.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.neutral aromatic: Kept minimal because donor–acceptor functionality and strong torsion sensitivity are inconsistent with a dominant neutral-aromatic LE mechanism.

working_memory.verifier_supplement_summary: Authoritative reviews and close-family precedents: f→0 near 90° torsion is expected for TICT/orbital decoupling, but decisive attribution requires additional hallmarks (large dipole increase, strong positive solvatochromism, dual emission, viscosity dependence, NTO/fragment CT analysis). In phenolic-OH Schiff-base/imine families, ICT often dominates unless those hallmarks are demonstrated. In heteroatom-rich C=N/N=N systems, dark states at 90° can also result from n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE, or state reordering.
working_memory.closure_justification_summary: Internal torsion-snapshot parsing established strong state reordering and S1 darkening near 90°, but internal CT/dipole surrogates are unavailable from the existing artifacts. Closure now requires new internal evidence that better aligns with verifier criteria (CT character/dipole change or experimentally motivated discriminators).
working_memory.pairwise_task_rationale: The key discriminator remains whether the orthogonal configuration is genuinely CT-like (TICT) or merely a dark state becoming S1 via reordering while a bright ICT/LE state persists higher. Existing artifacts cannot provide CT/dipole evidence; a new compute action (conformer dependence) is the most feasible next discriminative step within current registry constraints.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Conformer-bundle parsing shows a robust bright transition persists (often S2 with f~0.60–0.70) while S1 can become nearly dark (f~0.0015) across many accessible conformers, consistent with an ICT/LE bright manifold plus state-ordering effects. Baseline planar conformer remains S1-bright (f~0.385).

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: The dominant pattern across conformers is state reordering (dark S1 + bright higher state) without decisive CT hallmarks; in heteroatom-rich azo/imine systems this can reflect n→π* / symmetry-forbidden LE / assignment effects rather than CT-driven twisting.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Orthogonal torsion and some conformers access dark low-lying states (compatible with a TICT-accessible channel), but the computed evidence so far primarily indicates reordering rather than a uniquely CT-decoupled emissive minimum; decisive CT hallmarks (dipole change, NTO/fragment CT indices, solvatochromism/viscosity dependence) remain missing.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Phenolic OH and H-bond-like geometries are present, but there is no ESIPT-specific excited-state signature in available outputs; ESIPT remains a low-probability alternative.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Push–pull/heteroatom-rich scaffold and strong state-ordering dependence argue against a dominant neutral locally excited aromatic mechanism.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Conformer and torsion sampling consistently show a bright low-lying transition persists while the lowest state can become dark (state reordering). This matches an ICT/LE bright manifold with nearby dark states rather than uniquely requiring a CT-decoupled TICT emissive minimum.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Dark S1 + bright higher state across many geometries plus heteroatom-rich azo/imine motifs keeps open non-CT dark-state explanations (n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE, mixing/reordering) that we still cannot rule out with missing CT/NTO-type diagnostics.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Orthogonal-torsion darkening is compatible with TICT accessibility, but decisive TICT hallmarks (large dipole change, strong positive solvatochromism, dual emission, viscosity dependence, NTO/fragment CT metrics) remain absent; observed behavior is well-explained by reordering without proving CT-decoupling.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Phenolic OH and intramolecular H-bond-like geometries exist, but there is no ESIPT-specific signature (tautomer band/dual ESIPT emission) in available evidence.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Push–pull heteroatom-rich scaffold and strong geometry-dependent state ordering are inconsistent with a primarily neutral-aromatic LE mechanism.

working_memory.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: Uncertainty is dominated by lack of decisive state-character observables (NTO/particle–hole, transition charges, excited-state dipoles). The system robustly shows state reordering (dark S1 with bright higher state) across torsion and conformers, but this pattern alone cannot distinguish CT-driven TICT from non-CT dark states (n→π*, forbidden LE, mixing).
working_memory.capability_assessment: Microscopic computations/parsings can robustly establish torsion/conformer-dependent state ordering and provide some S0-level surrogates (ground-state dipole, Mulliken charges, MO files). However, the current workflow does not produce decisive CT diagnostics (NTO/transition densities/transition charges/excited-state dipoles), so discrimination must rely on weaker surrogates or external/experimental discriminators.
working_memory.stagnation_assessment: The route 'find NTO/transition-charge diagnostics in existing artifacts' is now conclusively stalled (confirmed absent). Progress is still possible by pivoting to supported surrogate extraction (dipole/charges) or by moving to external/experimental discriminators rather than repeating the same missing-observable search.
working_memory.contraction_reason: After confirming decisive CT diagnostics are absent from raw artifacts, the most informative low-cost internal step is to quantify available surrogates (S0 dipole + Mulliken charge patterns) across conformers to see whether dark S1 correlates with increased charge separation; this directly targets ICT vs unknown and indirectly informs TICT plausibility.
working_memory.information_gain_assessment: Medium: extracting/standardizing available dipole/charge surrogates across conformers could provide the only remaining internal discriminator for CT-likeness without new methods. If surrogates show no correlation, it strengthens the case for unknown/non-CT dark states; if strong correlation exists, it supports CT-channel/TICT-accessibility.
working_memory.gap_trend: Gap is shrinking on 'what is and is not available' (now clear) and on 'state ordering' (well established). Gap remains open on 'state character/CT attribution'; next step targets the best-available internal surrogate axis.

hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ICT: Upweighted: persistent bright low-lying transition across conformers with variable ordering supports an ICT/LE bright manifold rather than a uniquely TICT-dominated emissive minimum.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.unknown: Upweighted: confirmed absence of CT diagnostics means alternative dark-state origins flagged by verifier cannot be excluded; state reordering remains compatible with n→π*/forbidden LE.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.TICT: Downweighted: without NTO/dipole-change/solvatochromism evidence, orthogonal darkening is insufficient; inspection confirms missing decisive hallmarks.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ESIPT: Slightly upweighted but still low: H-bond-like geometries exist, yet no ESIPT photophysical signature is present.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.neutral aromatic: Kept minimal: donor–acceptor scaffold and strong geometry dependence argue against simple neutral LE behavior.

working_memory.verifier_supplement_summary: Authoritative reviews and close-family precedents: f→0 near 90° torsion is expected for TICT/orbital decoupling, but decisive attribution requires additional hallmarks (large dipole increase, strong positive solvatochromism, dual emission, viscosity dependence, NTO/fragment CT analysis). In phenolic-OH Schiff-base/imine families, ICT often dominates unless those hallmarks are demonstrated. In heteroatom-rich C=N/N=N systems, dark states at 90° can also result from n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE, or state reordering.
working_memory.closure_justification_summary: We have strong internal evidence for state reordering (dark lowest state + bright higher state) across torsion and conformers, and we now know decisive CT diagnostics are absent from raw artifacts. To close ICT vs unknown, we must test CT-likeness using the best available internal surrogates (dipole/charge patterns) or pivot to external experimental discriminators.
working_memory.pairwise_task_rationale: ICT vs unknown hinges on dark-state identity; since NTO/transition-charge diagnostics are absent, the remaining feasible internal discriminator is whether dark-S1 conformers show systematically larger dipoles/charge separation surrogates than bright-S1 conformers.

hypothesis_reweight_explanation.unknown: Upweighted from 0.33→0.36 because decisive CT/assignment diagnostics remain absent and the robust dark-S1/bright-S2 motif is compatible with n→π*/forbidden LE or mixing explanations.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.TICT: Downweighted from 0.18→0.15 because reordering explains the key f-collapse observations without requiring a CT-decoupled TICT state; no decisive TICT hallmarks are available.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ESIPT: Slightly upweighted from 0.05→0.06 because phenolic OH/H-bonding motifs are present across geometries, but still low due to lack of ESIPT-specific photophysical evidence.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.neutral aromatic: Unchanged at 0.01 because push–pull and strong geometry-dependent state ordering argue against a dominant neutral LE mechanism.

working_memory.verifier_supplement_summary: Authoritative reviews and close-family precedents: f→0 near 90° torsion is expected for TICT/orbital decoupling, but decisive attribution requires additional hallmarks (large dipole increase, strong positive solvatochromism, dual emission, viscosity dependence, NTO/fragment CT analysis). In phenolic-OH Schiff-base/imine families, ICT often dominates unless those hallmarks are demonstrated. In heteroatom-rich C=N/N=N systems, dark states at 90° can also result from n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE, or state reordering.
working_memory.closure_justification_summary: We now possess per-conformer S0 dipoles and per-state E/f that show robust dark-S1/bright-S2 reordering. Closure between ICT and unknown requires a targeted surrogate test: whether S1 darkness correlates with increased dipole (CT-likeness) and whether the reordering pattern is systematic in orbital transitions (consistent manifold) versus indicative of different state types (n→π*/forbidden LE).
working_memory.pairwise_task_rationale: ICT vs unknown hinges on dark-state identity; the only feasible internal discriminator with current artifacts is correlation of S1 darkness with S0 dipole/charge surrogates and systematic orbital-transition patterns across conformers.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Across the 10 conformers, the dominant pattern is a dark S1 with a strongly bright S2 (f~0.60–0.70) and highly systematic orbital-transition assignments (S1≈99→101, S2≈100→101), consistent with an ICT/LE bright manifold plus state reordering rather than a uniquely CT-decoupled TICT state.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Despite the very systematic reordering, we still lack decisive state-character diagnostics (NTOs, transition densities/charges, excited-state dipoles). In heteroatom-rich azo/imine systems, a consistently dark lowest state could still reflect n→π*/symmetry-forbidden LE; thus an alternative-dark-state bucket remains plausible.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Orthogonal-torsion darkening and availability of dark low-lying states are compatible with a TICT-accessible channel, but the observed behavior is well-explained by state reordering of two closely related π→π* configurations; decisive TICT hallmarks (large dipole change, strong positive solvatochromism/dual emission, NTO separation) remain unobserved.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Phenolic OH and repeated H-bond-like geometries exist, but no ESIPT-specific excited-state signature (tautomer band / characteristic dual emission) is available in current artifacts.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Push–pull scaffold with strong conformer/torsion-dependent state ordering is inconsistent with a primarily neutral locally excited aromatic mechanism dominating.

working_memory.gap_trend: Shrinking on state-ordering/manifold-identity; stalled on CT attribution due to missing decisive diagnostics, with partial hope via dipole availability check.

hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ICT: Upweighted because conformers show a persistent bright π→π* state (usually S2) with highly consistent transition assignment, while a dark state becomes S1 via systematic reordering—more consistent with an ICT/LE bright manifold plus dark-state competition than with uniquely CT-decoupled TICT.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.unknown: Held high because decisive CT-vs-n→π*/forbidden-LE diagnostics are absent; a consistently dark lowest state could still be non-CT in heteroatom-rich azo/imine systems.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.TICT: Downweighted because the strongest current signature is state reordering with closely related π→π* transition patterns, and we lack the verifier-required hallmarks (large dipole change/solvatochromism/dual emission/NTO separation).
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ESIPT: Slightly upweighted (still low) due to persistent intramolecular H-bond-like geometries, but no ESIPT photophysical hallmark is present.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.neutral aromatic: Kept low because donor–acceptor structure and strong geometry-dependent state ordering contradict a simple neutral aromatic LE picture.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Across conformers, the lowest state is often dark while a bright state (usually S2) persists with high oscillator strength; dominant transitions show a highly systematic two-manifold reordering (S1≈99→101; S2≈100→101), consistent with an ICT/LE bright manifold plus consistent state reordering rather than uniquely requiring a TICT CT-decoupled state.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Even with systematic reordering, we still lack decisive state-character diagnostics (NTO/particle–hole, excited-state dipoles/transition densities, transition charges). In heteroatom-rich azo/imine systems, a consistently dark lowest state could still be n→π* or symmetry-forbidden LE, so an alternative-dark-state bucket remains plausible.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Orthogonal-torsion darkening is compatible with TICT accessibility, but the conformer evidence is better explained as systematic state reordering between two closely related configurations; decisive TICT hallmarks (large dipole change, strong positive solvatochromism/dual emission, NTO separation) are absent.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Phenolic OH and H-bond-like geometries exist, but no ESIPT-specific excited-state signature (tautomer band/characteristic dual ESIPT emission) is available; remains a low-probability alternative pathway.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Strong push–pull functionality and pronounced conformer/torsion-dependent state ordering argue against a purely neutral locally-excited aromatic mechanism dominating.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic

working_memory.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: ICT is favored due to robust, highly systematic state reordering (dark S1 + bright S2) with consistent dominant transitions across conformers, but the dark state’s electronic type cannot be decisively assigned without CT/NTO/transition-density diagnostics or orbital-localization evidence; hence unknown remains substantial.
working_memory.capability_assessment: Microscopic capabilities can compute and parse vertical manifolds (E, f, state ordering) and dominant transitions across torsion and conformer bundles. However, decisive state-character tools (NTO/particle–hole, excited-state dipoles/transition densities, transition charges) are absent from artifacts. Attempts to use MO-localization surrogates are currently blocked because the inspection outputs do not expose MO-coefficient or per-MO population tables in a machine-usable way.
working_memory.stagnation_assessment: Not globally stagnant (7 rounds remain), but the specific route “MO-localization surrogate via inspect_raw_artifact_bundle” appears blocked by limited extractable observables. Switching to torsion-bundle dominant-transition consistency is a different, still-feasible discriminator.
working_memory.contraction_reason: Since CT/NTO and MO-localization diagnostics are unavailable, contract to the best available internal discriminator: whether dominant-transition manifold identity is consistent across torsion angles (including 90°), which can distinguish systematic π→π* reordering (supports ICT) from a change in state type (supports unknown).
working_memory.information_gain_assessment: Medium: parsing dominant transitions across the torsion pathway is low-cost and directly tests whether the conformer-derived systematic manifold identity persists at 90°; this is the best available internal discriminator short of missing CT/NTO metrics.
working_memory.gap_trend: Gap is shrinking on ‘systematic manifold identity’ (strong across conformers) and now will be tested across torsion; CT attribution remains structurally limited by missing diagnostics.

hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ICT: Slightly downweighted vs prior because the attempted MO-localization discriminator could not be executed; still top due to strong, systematic reordering across conformers with consistent dominant transitions.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.unknown: Slightly upweighted because the dark state’s electronic type remains unassigned and MO-localization/CT diagnostics remain unavailable; alternative dark-state origins remain plausible.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.TICT: Downweighted because key TICT decisives (dipole change/solvatochromism/dual emission/NTO) remain missing and current evidence is well explained by systematic reordering without invoking CT-decoupled emissive minima.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ESIPT: Held low but slightly supported by persistent H-bond-like geometries; still lacks ESIPT-specific photophysical signatures.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.neutral aromatic: Remains very low; observed donor–acceptor behavior and geometry-dependent state ordering are inconsistent with simple neutral LE dominance.

working_memory.verifier_supplement_summary: Authoritative reviews/precedents: f→0 near 90° torsion is compatible with TICT/orbital decoupling but not decisive; decisive criteria require large dipole change, strong positive solvatochromism, dual emission, viscosity dependence, and/or NTO/fragment CT analysis. In phenolic-OH Schiff-base/imine families, ICT often dominates unless those hallmarks are shown; in heteroatom-rich C=N/N=N systems, dark states can also arise from n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE, or state reordering.
working_memory.closure_justification_summary: Closure against 'unknown' requires showing the dark state is part of the same systematic π→π* manifold (not n→π*/forbidden LE). With MO-localization blocked, the next best internal surrogate is cross-geometry dominant-transition consistency (conformers + torsion angles, especially 90°).
working_memory.pairwise_task_rationale: The key ICT vs unknown discriminator is dark-state identity. Since CT/NTO and orbital-localization diagnostics are unavailable, the most feasible internal discriminator is whether dominant-transition patterns remain the same across torsion (including 90°), supporting systematic π→π* reordering (ICT) versus a change in state type (unknown).

Remaining uncertainty is whether the dark S1 is truly within the same π→π* manifold (ICT/LE competition) or a different state type (n→π*/forbidden LE) that current artifacts cannot decisively diagnose without NTO/transition-density/transition-charge or excited-state dipole data.

working_memory.stagnation_assessment: Not stagnant: the observable is being refined (from ‘darkening exists’ → ‘systematic reordering’ → ‘cross-geometry transition-family consistency’). The CT/NTO route is stalled by missing diagnostics, but dominant-transition consistency remains a viable internal route.
working_memory.contraction_reason: Since CT/NTO/excited-state dipole diagnostics are unavailable, focus on a single supported action that can still discriminate ICT vs unknown: whether the dark S1 keeps the same dominant-transition family across conformers as seen across torsion (especially at 90°).

hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ICT: Upweighted because torsion@90° dark S1 still shows dominant transitions into the same key virtual (101) and the overall picture matches systematic reordering seen across conformers (bright state persists in low manifold).
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.unknown: Downweighted slightly because the torsion@90° dominant transitions do not show an obvious change to a completely different excitation family; remains substantial due to lack of decisive state-type diagnostics.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.TICT: Downweighted because the observed torsion-induced darkening is well explained by reordering and lacks verifier-required decisive CT hallmarks (dipole change/solvatochromism/dual emission/NTO).
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ESIPT: Held because phenolic OH/H-bond-like geometries exist but no ESIPT-specific photophysical signature is available.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.neutral aromatic: Kept low because donor–acceptor structure and strong geometry dependence contradict a simple neutral LE mechanism.

working_memory.verifier_supplement_summary: Authoritative reviews/precedents: f→0 near 90° torsion is compatible with TICT/orbital decoupling but not decisive; decisive criteria require large dipole change, strong positive solvatochromism, dual emission, viscosity dependence, and/or NTO/fragment CT analysis. In phenolic-OH Schiff-base/imine families, ICT often dominates unless those hallmarks are shown; in heteroatom-rich C=N/N=N systems, dark states can also arise from n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE, or state reordering.
working_memory.closure_justification_summary: We now have dominant transitions across torsion (including 90°) showing systematic involvement of the same virtual orbital family. Next we must verify the same transition-family consistency across conformers; consistency supports ICT-with-systematic-reordering, while frequent switches to different virtuals/state families would support unknown.
working_memory.pairwise_task_rationale: ICT vs unknown hinges on dark-state identity; with CT/NTO and orbital-localization blocked, the best internal discriminator is whether the dominant-transition family of the dark S1 is consistent across conformer space and matches the torsion@90° dark S1.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Latest conformer-bundle parsing shows a highly systematic manifold: in conformers 2–10 the lowest state is dark (f~0.0015) with dominant transition ≈99→101, while a strongly bright state persists just above (typically S2, f~0.60–0.70) with dominant transition ≈100→101. This mirrors the torsion-snapshot picture (darkening by state ordering with the same key virtual orbital family), supporting an ICT/LE bright manifold with systematic reordering.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Even with systematic dominant-transition families, the physical nature of the dark state cannot be decisively typed as CT vs n→π*/symmetry-forbidden LE because decisive diagnostics are absent (NTO/particle–hole, transition densities/charges, excited-state dipoles, robust dipole-vs-geometry coverage). Thus an alternative-dark-state bucket remains plausible but is less favored given the strong manifold consistency.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Phenolic OH and repeated intramolecular H-bond-like geometry candidates exist in parseable geometry proxies, but no ESIPT-specific excited-state signature (tautomer band / characteristic dual ESIPT emission) is available in current artifacts.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Twist-associated S1 darkening is present, but internal evidence increasingly points to state ordering within a consistent π→π* transition family rather than decisive CT-decoupled TICT; verifier also states torsion-driven f→0 is not decisive without dipole/solvatochromism/dual-emission/NTO hallmarks (unavailable here).

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Push–pull heteroatom-rich scaffold and strong torsion/conformer-dependent state ordering argue against a primarily neutral locally excited aromatic mechanism dominating.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic

raw_response.diagnosis: Task: assess the likely AIE mechanism label (ICT/TICT/ESIPT/neutral aromatic/unknown) for the provided molecule. Working top1 is TICT, with top2 ICT. The structure is a donor–π–acceptor-like system with several rotatable single bonds linking aromatic/imine/azo-containing fragments, making a twist-enabled CT nonradiative channel plausible in solution and RIM (restriction of intramolecular motion) plausible upon aggregation—consistent with TICT-driven AIE. However, planarity_proxy is high (~0.98) in the selected conformer and the conjugated scaffold could remain largely planar, which would favor a more planar ICT (or LE) picture; thus TICT vs ICT is uncertain until we see whether S1 is strongly charge-transfer and whether low-energy torsional distortion correlates with S1 character. raw_response.diagnosis: Top1 is now ICT (0.41) with TICT as top2 (0.32). Latest evidence weakens the prior top1 (TICT): the selected conformer is highly planar (planarity_proxy~0.983), arguing against a pre-twisted ground-state geometry that would predispose TICT, and the microscopic baseline shows the lowest vertical excited state is already bright (E1=3.0895 eV, f=0.386; lowest_state_is_bright), which is more naturally consistent with a planar ICT/π→π* transition at the Franck–Condon geometry. This same evidence strengthens ICT as the challenger and elevates it to top1. An additional candidate affected is neutral aromatic, which is slightly downweighted because the donor–acceptor partition proxy (1.0) and bright S1 suggest significant polarity/CT character rather than purely local excitation. Key remaining discriminator between ICT vs TICT: whether there exists a lower-energy twisted S1 minimum (or strong torsion-dependent CT character) relative to the planar configuration; baseline vertical excitations do not test torsion dependence or relaxed excited-state minima. Verifier supplementation remains missing; closure justification is missing. Best next action is a bounded microscopic torsion-snapshot task focused on the single most plausible donor–acceptor dihedral to directly test torsion dependence of excitation energy/oscillator strength/CT proxies, which is the most discriminative internal evidence for ICT vs TICT at low cost.
raw_response.task_instruction: Run ONE Amesp microscopic action: `run_torsion_snapshots` on the most plausible conjugation-gating dihedral along the donor–acceptor linkage (aryl–C=N–N=C–PAH axis; choose the single highest-relevance dihedral near the C=N/N=C connection from the prepared structure). Generate ~7 snapshots across 0–180° (e.g., 0, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180). For each snapshot, compute the same vertical state window (S1–S5) and report: (i) S1 excitation energy and oscillator strength vs dihedral angle, (ii) whether the lowest state becomes dark near ~90° (a TICT hallmark), and (iii) any available simple CT/orbital-localization descriptors if supported by snapshot parsing.

raw_response.diagnosis: Top1=TICT (0.52), Top2=ICT (0.26). Latest evidence strengthens TICT: torsion snapshots on dihedral dih_10_11_12_13 show marked torsion sensitivity and a near-dark S1 at ~90° (f≈0.0014) relative to more emissive intermediate twists (e.g., 30–60° f≈0.13–0.15), which is a hallmark expectation for twist-activated CT/TICT channels. This challenges the prior ICT-leaning picture based on high ground-state planarity and a bright FC-point vertical S1 (baseline f≈0.386), which now reads as 'bright at FC, but can access dark twisted configurations'. One additional candidate affected is neutral aromatic, which is further disfavored because the excitation is not torsion-invariant/rigid-LE-like. raw_response.diagnosis: Round budget: 3/20 (18 remaining). Top1=TICT (0.56), Top2=ICT (0.24). Latest evidence (parse-only CT descriptor extraction) does not provide true CT metrics (NTO/charge-transfer indices missing), so it neither decisively strengthens nor decisively weakens TICT; however, it confirms the key photophysical discriminator remains: the ~90° torsion snapshot is strongly dark (f~0.0014) while nearby angles (e.g., 60°) are much brighter (f~0.147), supporting a twist-gated dark channel compatible with TICT. It also suggests potential state reordering (bright intensity not necessarily on S1 at all angles), which modestly supports the residual 'unknown' alternative and keeps ICT as a meaningful runner-up (bright FC-point state still plausible). Key remaining discriminator for TICT vs ICT: whether the darkened orthogonal geometry corresponds to a genuine CT-separated state versus a symmetry/selection-rule effect or state assignment artifact in a low-cost vertical snapshot model. Verifier supplementation: missing. Closure justification: collecting (internal) but currently blocked from being decisive by missing explicit CT diagnostics. Best next action now is Verifier, because internal routes cannot generate the missing CT descriptors from the existing bundle (parse-only reported them unavailable), so external precedent/criteria are the highest-leverage way to discriminate whether torsion-induced darkening in such D–A imine/azo-aryl systems is typically attributed to TICT/CT or to alternative effects.
raw_response.task_instruction: Verifier: For the molecule (SMILES: N#N=NCCOc4ccc(/C=N/N=c2c1ccccc1c3ccccc23)c(O)c4), help discriminate TICT vs ICT. Using literature/precedents on donor–acceptor Schiff-base/azo-aryl chromophores: (1) Is a strong oscillator-strength collapse near ~90° torsion around the D–A conjugation hinge typically diagnostic of TICT/CT decoupling (and what additional hallmarks are expected: solvatochromism, large dipole change, dual emission)? (2) What are common alternative explanations for near-orthogonal dark states in such systems (state reordering, n→π*, symmetry forbidden LE) and how to distinguish them from TICT? (3) Any close structural precedents (aryl–O–CH2–aryl with C=N–N=C / azo/imine linkage and phenolic OH) where twisting yields dark CT states vs retained ICT?

capability_lesson_candidates.recommended_contraction: Ask verifier specifically for literature patterns where ~90° torsion causes oscillator-strength collapse and how that maps to TICT vs non-CT dark states.

decision.information_gain_assessment: High expected gain from Verifier: external precedent can directly interpret whether torsion-to-90° darkening in comparable scaffolds is a reliable TICT signature and provide discriminators when CT metrics are absent.
decision.gap_trend: Gap shrinking on the torsion-response axis (darkening established), but stalled on the CT-attribution axis due to missing internal CT descriptors; needs external supplementation or new capability.

hypothesis_reweight_explanation.TICT: Slightly upweighted: torsion-induced near-dark state at ~90° remains the strongest mechanistic hallmark in the evidence; lack of CT metrics prevents a larger increase.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ICT: Slightly downweighted: bright FC-point S1 supports ICT locally, but cannot explain the pronounced orthogonal darkening as well as a twist-gated channel can.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.unknown: Held steady: state ordering/assignment variability and azo/imine complexity still allow alternative pathways not resolved by available CT descriptors.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ESIPT: Downweighted: no new H-bond/preorganization or proton-transfer signature emerged.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.neutral aromatic: Downweighted: strong torsion sensitivity and push–pull topology are inconsistent with a dominant neutral-LE mechanism.

decision.closure_justification_summary: Internal closure work established a strong torsion-gated darkening near ~90° on the key hinge (supports TICT accessibility), but explicit CT metrics could not be extracted from artifacts; external criteria/precedent are needed to justify TICT over ICT decisively.
decision.pairwise_task_rationale: ICT vs TICT hinges on whether orthogonal twisting produces a CT-decoupled dark state; internal torsion snapshots show darkening, but CT attribution is unresolved due to missing CT descriptors.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Central hinge torsion sweep shows a pronounced collapse of oscillator strength near 90° (S1 becomes nearly dark), consistent with access to a twisted, decoupled CT-like state; additional parse-only outputs do not disconfirm this.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Baseline relaxed-S0 vertical S1 is bright at the near-planar geometry, consistent with planar ICT at the Franck–Condon point; however, strong darkening upon orthogonalization weakens a purely planar ICT-only narrative.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Azo/imine motifs plus apparent state-ordering variability across torsion angles (e.g., bright state shifting among low-lying states) leaves room for alternative photophysics not captured by simple ICT/TICT labels.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Phenolic OH is present, but there is no supporting evidence of a preorganized intramolecular H-bond or ESIPT-specific excited-state signature in the current bundles.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Push–pull functionality and strong torsion sensitivity argue against a predominantly locally excited neutral-aromatic mechanism as the main class.

normalized_response.evidence_summary: Key internal evidence: (i) Baseline near-planar S0 geometry shows bright lowest vertical S1 (E~3.09 eV, f~0.386). (ii) Torsion sweep on a central conjugation hinge shows strong torsion sensitivity with near-dark lowest state at ~90° (f~0.0014), while other angles (e.g., 60°) remain bright (f~0.147). (iii) Attempted CT-descriptor extraction from the torsion snapshot bundle found that explicit CT/localization descriptor families were not available; only surrogates like dominant transitions, dipole, Mulliken charges, and state ordering were parsable.
normalized_response.main_gap: Decisive attribution of the ~90° dark state: genuine twisted charge-transfer (TICT) vs alternative dark-state origins (n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE, or state reordering/assignment artifacts).
normalized_response.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: Internal evidence robustly shows torsion-gated darkening near ~90° on a central hinge, but explicit CT character metrics are unavailable in the current artifact set; state reordering/assignment and alternative dark-state origins remain the main uncertainty between ICT-with-dark-channel vs bona fide TICT.
normalized_response.capability_assessment: Microscopic can produce baseline vertical excitations and torsion snapshots, and can parse limited surrogates from bundles. However, the requested CT/localization descriptors (NTO localization, particle–hole overlap, fragment charge difference) were not present in the torsion snapshot artifacts and cannot be generated via parse-only actions; excited-state relaxation is unsupported. Therefore, further internal work on 'CT confirmation' is stalled without changing tool capability.
normalized_response.stagnation_assessment: Potential stall detected for the specific route 'confirm CT character via descriptor extraction' because the platform reports those descriptor families are missing/unavailable in the bundle; continuing the same internal approach is unlikely to close the gap.
normalized_response.contraction_reason: Shift to Verifier because the key missing discriminator (is the dark orthogonal state genuinely CT/TICT?) cannot be established internally with available CT metrics; external precedent and discriminator criteria can meaningfully reduce ambiguity before spending additional internal compute.
normalized_response.information_gain_assessment: High expected gain from Verifier: external precedent can directly interpret whether torsion-to-90° darkening in comparable scaffolds is a reliable TICT signature and provide discriminators when CT metrics are absent.
normalized_response.gap_trend: Gap shrinking on the torsion-response axis (darkening established), but stalled on the CT-attribution axis due to missing internal CT descriptors; needs external supplementation or new capability.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Internal torsion sweep shows pronounced oscillator-strength collapse at ~90° on the key hinge (consistent with access to a twisted dark state), and verifier confirms this is a hallmark expected for TICT—but also stresses it is not decisive without dipole/NTO/solvatochromism evidence.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Baseline near-planar geometry has a bright lowest vertical excitation (FC-point consistency with planar ICT), and verifier provides close-family precedent that phenolic-OH Schiff-base/imine systems often remain ICT unless dual-emission/strong positive solvatochromism (or large dipole change) is demonstrated.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Verifier highlights plausible alternative origins of dark states in heteroatom-rich conjugated systems (state reordering, n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE) that cannot be excluded given missing NTO/CT metrics internally.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No internal ESIPT-specific observable was obtained; verifier notes phenolic OH can engage H-bonding but does not provide evidence that ESIPT dominates here.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Push–pull functionality plus strong torsion dependence argue against a primarily locally excited neutral-aromatic mechanism class.

raw_response.diagnosis: Round 4/20 (17 remaining). Top1=TICT (0.50), Top2=ICT (0.32). Verifier evidence is genuinely new and high-information for the current closure pair (TICT vs ICT), but it is mixed: it supports the interpretation that oscillator-strength collapse near ~90° is consistent with TICT/orbital decoupling, while simultaneously challenging decisiveness by emphasizing that oscillator-strength collapse alone is insufficient and that n→π*/symmetry-forbidden LE/state-reordering can mimic a dark orthogonal state—especially in heteroatom-rich C=N/N=N systems. Closure justification remains collecting: we now have sufficient verifier supplementation, but we still lack decisive internal observables (dipole change on excitation, NTO/fragment CT indices, solvatochromism/dual-emission/viscosity dependence) to close TICT over ICT decisively. The best next step is a new bounded internal follow-up that changes the observable to a discriminative metric we can actually compute with available tools (dipoles/charges across torsion snapshots) rather than repeating missing CT-descriptor extraction.
raw_response.task_instruction: Microscopic: run ONE action only: `extract_geometry_descriptors_from_bundle` on artifact_bundle_id=`round_02_torsion_snapshots`. Goal: compute and summarize per-snapshot geometry/electrostatic surrogates that can discriminate CT-like twisting vs non-CT dark states using available outputs. Return a concise table by angle (0,30,60,90,120,150,180): (i) ground-state dipole magnitude (already parsed) and any available per-snapshot charge-separation surrogate derived from Mulliken charges (e.g., define two fragments split across the hinge atoms [10-11-12-13] and report ΔQ between fragments if supported; otherwise report the max/min atomic charge and net charge on donor-side vs acceptor-side atom index sets), (ii) identify which state is the first bright state (max f within states 1–3) and its energy by angle (to track state reordering), and (iii) flag whether the darkening at 90° coincides with increased charge separation vs 60°/150°. If the tool cannot compute fragment charges automatically, at minimum output per-angle dipole and a note on the feasibility of computing fragment net charges from the available Mulliken list.

agent_task_instructions.microscopic: Execute exactly ONE registry action: `extract_geometry_descriptors_from_bundle` with artifact_bundle_id=`round_02_torsion_snapshots` (torsion_snapshots, source_round=2). In the returned summary, include: per-angle ground-state dipole; any available geometry descriptors; and, if supported by the extractor, fragment/net-charge summaries across a user-defined fragment split across the hinge (use dihedral atoms [10,11,12,13] as the cut). Additionally, use the existing parsed state_window=[1,2,3] data to report per-angle which state is brightest (max f) and its energy, to explicitly characterize state reordering.

raw_response.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: Main uncertainty is attribution: the observed ~90° oscillator-strength collapse supports access to a twisted dark state, but verifier evidence shows this can arise from classical TICT/CT decoupling or from alternative dark-state origins (n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE, or state reordering), and our internal bundle lacks NTO/CT indices. We need a discriminative observable we can compute (dipole/charge-separation vs angle; or experimental solvatochromism/viscosity tests if available externally) to choose TICT vs ICT decisively.
raw_response.capability_assessment: Verifier supplementation is now sufficient and materially clarifies what would count as decisive for TICT vs ICT (dipole change, positive solvatochromism, dual emission, viscosity dependence, NTO/fragment CT). Internally, explicit CT/NTO descriptors are unavailable from existing torsion artifacts (parse-only limitation), but we do have per-snapshot ground-state dipoles and Mulliken charges; a geometry/charge-separation surrogate analysis across torsion angles is a feasible next discriminator within current microscopic capabilities.
raw_response.stagnation_assessment: No global stagnation: the route 'extract CT descriptors' is stalled (missing descriptor families), but we are changing the observable to charge/dipole/bright-state tracking across torsion, which is a different feasible route.
raw_response.contraction_reason: Do not retry missing CT/NTO descriptor extraction. Instead, contract to computable discriminators from existing artifacts (dipoles, Mulliken-charge-derived separation, explicit bright-state tracking for reordering) aligned with verifier-stated decisive criteria.
raw_response.evidence_summary: Internal: baseline FC geometry has bright lowest vertical S1 (f~0.386). Torsion sweep on hinge dihedral shows near-dark lowest state at ~90° (f~0.0014) while other angles are bright (e.g., 60° f~0.147). Internal CT/NTO descriptors are missing from artifacts (cannot be generated post hoc). Verifier: authoritative reviews state TICT typically shows ~90° orthogonalization with near-zero oscillator strength plus large dipole increase and strong positive solvatochromism/dual emission; alternative dark states (n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE, state reordering) can mimic darkness and require dipole/NTO/solvatochromism/viscosity tests to distinguish. Close-family precedent in phenolic-OH Schiff-base/imine systems often favors ICT unless TICT hallmarks are demonstrated.
raw_response.main_gap: Decisive attribution of the ~90° dark state: is it a classical TICT/CT-decoupled state or an alternative dark-state origin (n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE, or state reordering) given missing NTO/fragment CT indices and no solvatochromism/viscosity evidence.
raw_response.information_gain_assessment: Verifier gain is high and new: it upgrades the closure criteria and introduces credible challenger-family precedent plus alternative explanations. Next-step internal gain is medium-high if we can quantify dipole/charge-separation vs torsion and explicitly document state reordering of the brightest state.
raw_response.gap_trend: Gap is shrinking in terms of knowing what decisive evidence is needed; it remains open because the decisive observables (NTO/CT indices, excited-state dipole change, solvatochromism/viscosity dependence) are not yet available. Switching to dipole/charge-separation surrogates is expected to reduce the attribution gap.

normalized_response.verifier_supplement_summary: Authoritative reviews and close-family precedents: (i) f→0 near 90° torsion is expected for TICT/orbital decoupling, but decisive attribution requires additional hallmarks (large dipole increase, strong positive solvatochromism, dual emission, viscosity dependence, NTO/fragment CT analysis). (ii) In phenolic-OH Schiff-base/imine families, ICT often dominates unless those TICT hallmarks are demonstrated. (iii) In heteroatom-rich C=N/N=N systems, dark states at 90° can also result from n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE, or state reordering.
normalized_response.closure_justification_summary: We have strong internal evidence for torsion-gated darkening at ~90° and now have verifier-provided decisive criteria for TICT vs ICT; however, we still lack the specific discriminators (dipole change/CT indices/solvatochromism/viscosity dependence) needed to close. raw_response.diagnosis: Round 6/20 (15 remaining). Top1=TICT (0.40), Top2=ICT (0.34). Latest evidence (parsed torsion-snapshot state manifold) weakens the decisiveness of the prior TICT-leaning interpretation: the ~90° geometry indeed yields a very dark S1 (f≈0.0014), but the same snapshot also shows a very bright higher state (S4, f≈0.526 at 4.286 eV), indicating strong state reordering rather than a global oscillator-strength collapse. This pushes probability mass toward the 'unknown' bucket because verifier-highlighted alternatives (state reordering, n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE) become more plausible without CT/NTO/dipole-change evidence. ICT remains a close runner-up because at several non-orthogonal angles the lowest state is bright (S1 bright at 30°, 60°, 120°, 150°), consistent with an accessible planar/near-planar bright ICT-like manifold at/near the FC region. Key remaining discriminator between TICT vs ICT: whether twisting induces genuine CT character (large dipole change/CT localization) versus merely swapping in a dark non-CT state as S1 while a bright LE/ICT state remains at higher index; current evidence supports the latter possibility. Verifier supplementation is sufficient (mixed) and closure justification is still collecting (internal), now specifically needing an internal surrogate for CT character or dipole-change vs torsion (not currently available from the torsion bundle). Chosen next action: microscopic, to extract whatever electrostatic/geometric surrogates are available in-registry (without inventing unsupported fragment-charge workflows) to test for dipole/charge-separation changes with torsion that would support TICT over state-reordering-only.

capability_lesson_candidates.recommended_contraction: Split: use parse_snapshot_outputs for f/E vs angle (done), then use extract_geometry_descriptors_from_bundle only for whatever dipole/geometry descriptors are natively supported; do not require fragment net-charge aggregation unless a dedicated registry action exists.

hypothesis_reweight_explanation.TICT: Downweighted slightly because the new multi-state parsing shows strong state reordering (bright higher states persist at 90°), making S1 darkening alone less diagnostic of classical TICT.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ICT: Slightly upweighted/maintained because multiple angles retain a bright lowest state (S1 bright), consistent with an ICT-like bright manifold at/near planar geometries.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.unknown: Upweighted because the explicit presence of dark S1 with bright S2/S4 at several angles matches verifier-flagged alternative explanations (state reordering, n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE) that we cannot exclude without CT hallmarks.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ESIPT: Kept low because no ESIPT-specific signatures have been produced internally and current observables are dominated by torsion/state-ordering effects.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.neutral aromatic: Further downweighted because behavior is highly torsion-sensitive and involves strong donor–acceptor/heteroatom features inconsistent with simple LE neutral-aromatic photophysics.

raw_response.verifier_supplement_summary: Authoritative reviews and close-family precedents: f→0 near 90° torsion is expected for TICT/orbital decoupling, but decisive attribution requires additional hallmarks (large dipole increase, strong positive solvatochromism, dual emission, viscosity dependence, NTO/fragment CT analysis). In phenolic-OH Schiff-base/imine families, ICT often dominates unless those hallmarks are demonstrated. In heteroatom-rich C=N/N=N systems, dark states at 90° can also result from n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE, or state reordering.
raw_response.closure_justification_summary: Parsed torsion snapshots show that near-90° darkening is accompanied by a bright higher state (state reordering), so closure now requires an additional internal discriminator aligned with verifier criteria—ideally dipole/charge-separation change vs torsion from supported geometry/electrostatic descriptors.
raw_response.pairwise_task_rationale: We must distinguish classical TICT (twist-induced CT with large dipole change) from ICT with state reordering (dark S1 but bright higher LE/ICT state). The newly parsed multi-state f/E trends show reordering; next we need dipole/electrostatic surrogates vs torsion from a single supported extraction action.

decision.diagnosis: Round 6/20 (15 remaining). Top1=TICT (0.40), Top2=ICT (0.34). Latest evidence (parsed torsion-snapshot state manifold) weakens the decisiveness of the prior TICT-leaning interpretation: the ~90° geometry indeed yields a very dark S1 (f≈0.0014), but the same snapshot also shows a very bright higher state (S4, f≈0.526 at 4.286 eV), indicating strong state reordering rather than a global oscillator-strength collapse. This pushes probability mass toward the 'unknown' bucket because verifier-highlighted alternatives (state reordering, n→π*, symmetry-forbidden LE) become more plausible without CT/NTO/dipole-change evidence. ICT remains a close runner-up because at several non-orthogonal angles the lowest state is bright (S1 bright at 30°, 60°, 120°, 150°), consistent with an accessible planar/near-planar bright ICT-like manifold at/near the FC region. Key remaining discriminator between TICT vs ICT: whether twisting induces genuine CT character (large dipole change/CT localization) versus merely swapping in a dark non-CT state as S1 while a bright LE/ICT state remains at higher index; current evidence supports the latter possibility. Verifier supplementation is sufficient (mixed) and closure justification is still collecting (internal), now specifically needing an internal surrogate for CT character or dipole-change vs torsion (not currently available from the torsion bundle). Chosen next action: microscopic, to extract whatever electrostatic/geometric surrogates are available in-registry (without inventing unsupported fragment-charge workflows) to test for dipole/charge-separation changes with torsion that would support TICT over state-reordering-only.
decision.task_instruction: Microscopic (ONE registry action): `extract_geometry_descriptors_from_bundle` on artifact_bundle_id=`round_02_torsion_snapshots`. Request any available descriptor_scope entries that include dipole and charge-related summaries if supported by the registry (do NOT require fragment net-charge aggregation). Goal: report per-angle S0 dipole magnitude/vector if present (or explicitly report unavailable per snapshot), and any geometry descriptors that correlate with charge separation (if available). Keep the output keyed by angle so we can compare 60° vs 90° vs 120° for dipole changes aligned with TICT expectations.

agent_task_instructions.microscopic: Execute exactly ONE Amesp registry action: `extract_geometry_descriptors_from_bundle` with artifact_bundle_id=`round_02_torsion_snapshots` (torsion_snapshots, source_round=2). Request descriptor_scope that includes any available dipole/electrostatics and basic geometry descriptors (do not request excited-state parsing or fragment charge aggregation). Return per-snapshot (per-angle) availability and values; explicitly flag which angles have dipole info and provide those values if present.

decision.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: Even though S1 becomes nearly dark at ~90° torsion, parse results show a bright higher state persists (strong state reordering). Without CT/NTO or dipole-change evidence across torsion, the ~90° dark S1 cannot be uniquely attributed to classical TICT; it may reflect a different dark state becoming S1 while an LE/ICT-like bright state remains at S2–S4.
decision.capability_assessment: Internal torsion-snapshot parsing is now sufficient to diagnose state reordering explicitly. However, decisive CT attribution (NTO/fragment charge difference/particle-hole overlap; excited-state dipole changes) remains unavailable from current torsion artifacts. The best remaining internal step is to extract any available S0 dipole/geometry surrogates vs torsion via registry-supported geometry-descriptor extraction (without bundling unsupported brightness/fragment-charge tasks).
decision.stagnation_assessment: No stagnation: latest round added a new discriminative observable (explicit multi-state f/E vs angle), changing the interpretation from 'simple S1 darkening' to 'state reordering with bright higher state'. Next step changes observable again to electrostatic/geometry surrogates that align with verifier criteria.
decision.contraction_reason: Avoid repeating unavailable CT/NTO descriptor extraction and avoid unsupported multi-part microscopic requests. Use a single supported geometry-descriptor extraction to seek dipole/electrostatics vs torsion as a proxy discriminator.

raw_response.diagnosis: Round 7/20 (14 remaining). Top1=TICT (0.33) with Top2=ICT (0.32); 'unknown' is now essentially tied (0.32) because the latest microscopic evidence shows that the desired electrostatic/CT surrogates cannot be extracted from the torsion-snapshot artifacts (dipole, electrostatics, per-atom Mulliken charges are missing), while the parsed excited-state manifold indicates substantial state reordering (dark S1 but bright higher states at several angles). This weakens the decisiveness of the prior TICT-leaning interpretation: S1 darkening near 90° remains compatible with TICT orbital decoupling, but the presence of bright higher states plus missing CT hallmarks leaves ICT-with-reordering and other dark-state mechanisms viable. Key remaining discriminator for TICT vs ICT is whether the orthogonal configuration corresponds to a bona fide CT state (large dipole/charge separation, positive solvatochromism, dual emission/viscosity dependence) versus a non-CT dark state that becomes S1 via reordering. Verifier supplementation remains sufficient (mixed); closure justification remains collecting because internal decisive CT/dipole evidence is still missing. Best next action is to run a new microscopic calculation route that can directly output CT/dipole/NTO-like diagnostics (or at least better state character surrogates) rather than further parse-only attempts on the existing torsion bundle.
raw_response.task_instruction: Microscopic: execute exactly ONE registry-backed action: `run_conformer_bundle` on the prepared structure to generate a small set of low-energy conformers (e.g., up to 10) and compute the same vertical excited-state window (S1–S5) for each conformer. Goal: test whether any low-energy conformer naturally accesses a more twisted donor–acceptor geometry with a consistently low oscillator strength lowest state and whether the bright state remains higher (state-reordering pattern), which would support 'dark-channel/state-reordering' vs a stabilized TICT-like minimum. Return: for each conformer, (i) key torsion angle for the previously identified hinge (dih_10_11_12_13 if available), (ii) lowest-state energy and oscillator strength, (iii) first bright state index/energy/f.

agent_task_instructions.microscopic: Run ONE action only: `run_conformer_bundle` using the existing prepared structure. Request a small conformer set (<=10) and vertical excitations for states 1–5 for each conformer. In the summary, report per conformer: (a) the hinge dihedral value corresponding to dih_10_11_12_13 if the tool can map it, otherwise report the most relevant rotatable dihedral value; (b) E/f for S1; (c) first bright state index + E + f; (d) indicate if lowest state is dark while higher state is bright (state reordering flag).

raw_response.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: Current uncertainty is dominated by inability to verify CT character: both CT/NTO descriptors and even simple electrostatic surrogates (dipole, Mulliken charges) were not present in the torsion-snapshot artifacts, while state reordering is clearly present. Thus, S1 darkening at ~90° cannot be uniquely attributed to classical TICT.
raw_response.capability_assessment: Verifier supplementation is sufficient and clarifies decisive discriminators. Internally, parse-only extraction from torsion snapshots cannot provide CT/dipole evidence because the relevant descriptor families are absent from artifacts. A new bounded microscopic computation (rather than further parsing) is needed to change the available observables.
raw_response.stagnation_assessment: Not globally stagnant (round budget ample), but the internal route 'extract CT/dipole surrogates from existing torsion bundle' is stalled due to missing artifact fields; new computations are required to progress.
raw_response.contraction_reason: Further parse-only attempts on the same torsion bundle are unlikely to add CT attribution evidence; switch to a single supported compute action that explores conformational space and characterizes whether dark/bright ordering is robust across low-energy structures.
raw_response.evidence_summary: Latest microscopic extraction (`extract_geometry_descriptors_from_bundle` on round_02_torsion_snapshots) succeeded but returned only limited geometry proxies (intramolecular H-bond candidates, local planarity proxy). Requested dipole/electrostatics/bond metrics/atomic Mulliken charges were not available in the artifact files, so no torsion-dependent dipole/charge-separation surrogate could be computed.
raw_response.main_gap: Decisive CT attribution for the dark near-orthogonal state: obtain an observable linked to charge separation (dipole change, NTO/fragment CT indices, or experimental solvatochromism/viscosity dependence) to distinguish classical TICT from ICT with state reordering or other dark-state mechanisms.
raw_response.information_gain_assessment: Medium: conformer-bundle excitations can show whether the dark/bright ordering is robust across accessible geometries and whether any low-energy twisted conformer looks like a stabilized dark CT channel; it still may not provide CT indices but changes the observable beyond the stalled parse-only route.
raw_response.gap_trend: Stalled on CT-attribution from existing artifacts; expected to resume shrinking once a new compute route provides additional observables across conformers.
